key_insight: The paper shows that sparse, physics-augmented neural networks can be turned into closed-form constitutive laws, enabling end-to-end optimization of both material composition and structural topology in multimaterial 3D printing.
engineering_relevance: This provides a practical route to design soft robotic and graded-material parts without relying on slow trial-and-error prototyping, while keeping gradients exact for optimization.
method: Fit experimental data with sparsified physics-augmented neural networks to learn composition-aware hyperelastic behavior.
Extract symbolic constitutive laws so the model can be differentiated exactly in an adjoint/FEniCSx finite-element workflow.
Couple material-distribution optimization with topology optimization under stretch and contact-performance constraints.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports application to soft robotic grippers and concurrent optimization of anisotropic contact response, topology, and material distribution under non-failure stretch constraints.
watch_item: The approach depends on high-quality experimental data and careful symbolic model extraction; if the learned constitutive law is poorly identified, optimization may become unreliable.
paper_title: Towards end-to-end optimization in multimaterial 3D printing
